user_journeys.step: **[Journey 1: Real-time Monitoring]** The user enters the "War Room" and views the "Ghost Architecture" idle state. Upon a task trigger, they watch the **coordinator (brain-08)** illuminate, followed by a sequence of satellite brains lighting up via WebSocket events [1, 2]. The user observes the **Hybrid Flow** animations to track the logic progression through the dependency graph, providing **immediate feedback** on the orchestration's health [3, 4].

**[Journey 2: Post-Mortem Analysis]** After the `task_completed` event, the system enters "Cooldown Mode." The user uses keyboard shortcuts ([V] or [R]) to toggle through the **Ghost Trace** history stack, reviewing snapshots of specific brain states stored in the `brainStore` [5, 6]. This allows the user to bridge the **Gulf of Evaluation** by interpreting how the final state was reached without needing to restart the process [7, 8].

pain_points: **[Cognitive Overload in High-Concurrency]** With 24 brains emitting simultaneous events, the user may experience a "firehose effect." If the **visual hierarchy** does not distinguish between primary path illumination and secondary status updates, the user will struggle to **scan** the most critical information [9, 10].

**[Ambiguous Signifiers]** If the "niche colors" used for animated edges are not clearly mapped to specific status meanings (e.g., data transfer vs. simple trigger), the user might experience a **mistake** in their mental model, incorrectly interpreting the orchestration flow [1, 8, 11].
opportunities: **[Natural Mapping of the Star Topology]** Positioning the coordinator (brain-08) as the central hub creates a **natural mapping** that matches the user's conceptual model of a "controller and satellites" [12, 13]. This reduces the time needed to understand the system structure [10].

**[Progressive Disclosure via Side Panel]** By using the **shadcn/ui Sheet** for live-binding to detailed brain states, the canvas remains minimalist. This follows the principle of **progressive disclosure**, keeping the main interface clean while providing deep-dive data on-demand [14, 15].
brain-02-ux-research.research_methodology: The research approach will utilize **Evaluative Research** through **Heuristic Evaluation**, specifically testing against Nielsen’s principles of **Visibility of System Status** and **Error Prevention** [17, 18]. We will conduct **Guerrilla Testing** with 5 users to identify approximately 85% of usability issues related to the visualization's 60fps performance and cognitive load [19-21]. Finally, we will apply **Assumption Mapping** to ensure the "Star Topology" matches the target audience's mental model of AI orchestration [22].

screen_flows.flow: **[NexusCanvas (Idle) → NexusCanvas (Active):]** The user views the blueprint-style nodes (20% opacity); a WebSocket signal triggers a status change in the `brainStore`, causing a node to transition from "Ghost" to "Illuminated" with a **neon glow signifier** [1, 2].

**[NexusCanvas (Active) → Side Panel (Detailed):]** The user clicks a specific node; the **Side Panel** slides out (fixed-right) to show real-time WS event logs and configuration, providing **contextual help** without losing the visual state of the DAG [5, 16].

brain-03-ui-design.visual_language: A Cyber-Blueprint War Room aesthetic that is technical, focused, and high-contrast. The mood is one of real-time orchestration, utilizing a dark mode environment to make illuminated AI "brain" states the primary focal point [1, 2]. The tone is professional and precise, following a "form follows function" philosophy where visual elements only exist to communicate data insights or state changes [3-5].

color_palette.primary: #64FFDA (Neon Cyan for active hub and illuminated nodes) [6]
color_palette.secondary: #8892B0 (Muted Slate for "Ghost" idle states and secondary edges) [7]
color_palette.accent: #FF3D00 (Vivid Red-Orange for error states and high-priority alerts) [6, 8]
color_palette.background: #0B0C10 (Deep Obsidian; avoids pure black to prevent OLED smearing) [7, 9]

typography.heading: Inter, 700 Bold [10, 11]
typography.mono: JetBrains Mono (for Brain IDs and real-time WebSocket event logs) [10]

brain-03-ui-design.spacing_system: 8px Base Grid. All margins, paddings, and layout positions follow a strictly mathematical scale (4-8-16-24-32-48-64) to ensure visual harmony and eliminate layout shifts [13-15].

component_hierarchy.name: **NexusBrainNode
component_hierarchy.description: ** An Atomic "Molecule" component with five distinct states: Blueprint (dashed, 20% opacity), Active (illuminated neon glow), Error (pulsing red-orange), Cooldown (solidified), and Disabled [16-19]. It includes a non-draggable container to prevent accidental layout shifts during high-frequency WebSocket updates [Brief].

component_hierarchy.name: **HybridFlowEdge
component_hierarchy.description: ** An animated connection component that uses "Meaningful Motion" to communicate data direction [3, 20]. In live mode, it features a moving neon "data pulse"; in Cooldown mode, it transitions to a solidified "Data-Latched" state with a permanent glow to show historical dependency [135, Brief].

component_hierarchy.name: **EventSidePanel
component_hierarchy.description: ** A right-aligned fixed "Organism" (shadcn/ui Sheet) that live-binds to the brainStore [7, Brief]. It uses a strict visual hierarchy with larger headings for KPIs and monospaced text for raw event streams, ensuring that the most important orchestration insights are visible within 3 seconds [21-23].

design_principles: **Hierarchy through Illumination:** Color and glow are reserved strictly for active orchestration; idle elements remain in a grayscale "blueprint" mode to reduce cognitive load and highlight what matters in real-time [2, 21, 24, 25].

**Redundant State Communication:** No state is communicated by color alone. Every brain state (Active, Idle, Error) is reinforced by a combination of unique color, distinct icons, and descriptive text labels to ensure accessibility for all users [26-28].

**Motion as Communication:** Animations are restricted to 100–300ms durations and must serve a functional purpose—either providing feedback for a WebSocket event or orienting the user within the star topology [3, 20, 29, 30]. Every animation respects the `prefers-reduced-motion` guard to prevent physical discomfort [31, 32].

brain-04-frontend.framework: Next.js 16 (App Router) + React 19. This choice leverages React Server Components (RSC) to fetch the initial "Ghost Architecture" blueprint via `GET /api/brains`, minimizing client-side JS for the base state [1, 2]. React 19's optimized rendering and improved Suspense boundaries handle high-frequency WebSocket (WS) bursts without blocking the main thread [3-5].

pages: **The Nexus War Room**: Main viewport containing the `NexusCanvas`. It initiates the WS connection and subscribes the `brainStore` to incoming orchestration events [17].
**Brain Deep-Dive (Side Panel)**: A fixed right-side panel that live-binds to `useBrainState(id)`. It displays real-time execution logs and "Ghost Trace" history snapshots [8, 18].
layout: **NexusLayout**: Implements a global **Performance Guard**, providing a `prefers-reduced-motion` context to disable intensive neon animations for sensitive users [19-21].
**War Room Header**: A persistent HUD showing the overall orchestration health and a keyboard shortcut legend (`[Enter]`, `[V]`, `[R]`) for "Cooldown Mode" navigation [22].
shared: **NexusCanvas (@xyflow/react)**: Implements the **dagre radial layout**. To prevent layout shift, node positions are calculated once during the "Ghost" load and fixed (`draggable: false`). WS updates target only the `data` property of nodes via **Zustand selectors** [11, 23, 24].

**BrainNode**: A custom React Flow node. It uses **memoization** to prevent re-renders unless its specific `BrainState` in the Map changes [25, 26]. It incorporates `nodrag` and `nopan` classes on internal interactive elements to ensure canvas stability [27].

brain-04-frontend.state_management:
Zustand 5 (Client State) + TanStack Query v5 (Server State).
- **TanStack Query** manages the initial brain configuration and "Ghost Architecture" loading state, providing automatic caching and synchronization [6, 7].
- **Zustand with Immer middleware** manages the `brainStore` using a `Map<brainId, BrainState>` for **O(1) lookups**, ensuring 60fps performance during 24 simultaneous brain events [8, 9].
- The `historyStack` for "Ghost Trace" is implemented as an array within Zustand, allowing power users to perform state-scrubbing via the **Side Panel** [10, 11].
brain-04-frontend.styling_approach:
Tailwind 4 + shadcn/ui + CSS Custom Properties.
- **Visual Hierarchy**: Idle nodes use `opacity-20` and `border-dashed`. Active states utilize **Tailwind 4 arbitrary values** for neon glows (e.g., `shadow-[0_0_15px_var(--brain-color)]`) [12, 13].
- **Niche Colors**: Brain-specific themes are injected via **CSS Custom Properties** (e.g., `--brain-08-hub`), ensuring a scalable design system that supports dark mode and high-contrast accessibility [12, 14].
brain-04-frontend.routing_strategy:
Next.js App Router with Dynamic Parallel Routes.
- `app/nexus/page.tsx`: The primary "War Room" canvas.
- `app/nexus/@sidepanel/[brainId]/page.tsx`: Uses **Parallel Routes** to render the **shadcn/ui Sheet** for a specific brain without unmounting the canvas, preserving the WS connection [15, 16].
performance_targets: **Target 1: 60fps UI Responsiveness.** Achieved by batching WS updates using `requestAnimationFrame` (RAF) and moving heavy "Ghost Trace" history processing to a **Web Worker** if calculations exceed 16ms [26, 31, 32].

**Target 2: CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift) < 0.1.** Node positions are latched post-Dagre calculation; visual state transitions (illumination) use `transform` and `opacity` exclusively to stay on the **GPU compositor** [21, 23, 33, 34].
build_tools: **Vite (via Next.js Turbo)**: Provides instant HMR (Hot Module Replacement) for rapid development of complex canvas shaders and animations [28].
**ESLint (Flat Config v9)**: Enforces `exhaustive-deps` to prevent stale closures in WS event listeners and `no-explicit-any` for strict TypeScript compliance [29, 30].

brain-06-qa-devops.testing_strategy:
Adopte un enfoque de Test Pyramid, priorizando un 70% de pruebas unitarias para la lógica de estado, un 20% de integración para el flujo de datos y un 10% de E2E para los flujos críticos del "war room" [1]. La calidad debe ser una responsabilidad compartida de todo el equipo y no una fase final [2]. El objetivo principal es mantener la estabilidad técnica (baja tasa de fallos en cambios) sin sacrificar la velocidad de entrega, apuntando a métricas de rendimiento "Elite" (despliegues múltiples veces al día) [3, 4]. Se implementará un enfoque de "Shift-left", integrando pruebas de seguridad y performance desde las etapas iniciales del diseño [5, 6]. | Se utilizará Vitest para validar la lógica pura del `brainStore` (Zustand) y las mutaciones realizadas con Immer. Se deben testear los selectores de Map específicos para garantizar que no existan re-renders innecesarios que causen layout shifts [Brief]. Es crítico probar de forma aislada la función de layout radial de dagre para asegurar posiciones de nodos estables [Brief]. Use "Seams" (costuras) para romper dependencias complejas y permitir el testeo de la lógica de orquestación de los 24 cerebros sin efectos secundarios [7, 8]. | El foco principal será el Contract Testing entre los eventos de WebSocket y el almacenamiento global del Nexus [6]. Se deben validar los puntos de integración entre Zustand y React Flow, específicamente que la "iluminación" de los nodos se active correctamente mediante el `brainState` [Brief]. Utilice la metodología de los "Three Amigos" (PO, Dev, QA) para definir los criterios de aceptación del flujo de orquestación mediante ejemplos concretos [9]. Se testeará la persistencia del `historyStack` (Ghost Trace) para asegurar que las capturas de estado por evento de WS sean íntegras [Brief].

**E2E_TESTING**: Utilice Playwright para automatizar los flujos de usuario de mayor valor: 1. Carga inicial de la **"Ghost Architecture"** desde `/api/brains`. 2. Transición visual en tiempo real durante la ejecución (iluminación de nodos y animación de aristas). 3. Persistencia de datos en el **"Cooldown Mode"** y funcionamiento de los **shortcuts de teclado** ([Enter]/[V]/[R]) [Brief]. Se incluirán **pruebas de regresión de performance** (Cuadrante 4) para verificar el mantenimiento de **60fps** bajo carga de 24 eventos simultáneos y el respeto a la guardia de `prefers-reduced-motion` [10-12].
brain-06-qa-devops.ci_cd_pipeline:
Implemente un flujo de Trunk-Based Development, donde los desarrolladores integren sus cambios a la rama principal diariamente mediante ramas de vida corta (< 2 días) [13, 14]. El Deployment Pipeline automatizado constará de:
1.  **Stage de Commit**: Linting, SAST (análisis estático de seguridad) y pruebas unitarias [15, 16].
2.  **Stage de Aceptación**: Despliegue en un entorno efímero, pruebas de integración y E2E [15].
3.  **Performance Check**: Pruebas de carga automáticas (ej. con k6) para validar latencias de renderizado de la DAG [12, 17].
Utilice **Feature Flags** para separar el despliegue técnico del lanzamiento de negocio, permitiendo "dark launches" del Nexus [1, 18].
brain-06-qa-devops.monitoring: Adopte principios de Observabilidad, utilizando eventos estructurados de alta cardinalidad para rastrear cada `brainId` individualmente a través de la orquestación [19, 20]. Defina SLOs (Service Level Objectives) claros, como: "El 99.9% de los eventos de WebSocket deben procesarse y renderizarse en < 16ms" para garantizar los 60fps [11, 21]. Implemente trazabilidad distribuida para seguir el flujo desde el Coordinador (brain-08) hacia los satélites [22, 23]. Use notificaciones proactivas para alertar sobre estados de error en los cerebros antes de que el usuario lo reporte [24].
brain-06-qa-devops.deployment_strategy: La infraestructura se gestionará como código (IaC), asegurando entornos de staging y producción idénticos mediante contenedores [25, 26]. Se priorizarán los Phoenix Servers (recrear en lugar de parchear) para evitar el "configuration drift" [27, 28]. El despliegue a producción será mediante lanzamientos graduales por porcentaje (1% → 10% → 100%) para limitar el radio de explosión ante posibles fallos en la visualización en tiempo real [29]. Todo cambio en infraestructura debe pasar por el pipeline, prohibiendo cambios manuales vía SSH [30].
